planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in humus-rich, well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in shade to partial shade; acuminate epimedium spreads slowly by short rhizomes to form a dense, weed-suppressing groundcover. It is one of the largest-flowered epimediums and produces unusually long, graceful flower spurs.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in shade to partial shade; acuminate epimedium is a Chinese species prized among epimedium collectors for its exceptionally long, elegant flower spurs — among the longest of any epimedium species — which give the flowers a dramatic, almost exotic appearance in the spring garden. The flowers come in combinations of white, purple, and pale violet with contrasting inner petals, and the unusually long curved spurs trail beneath like the limbs of a spider or a shuttlecock in flight. The foliage is semi-evergreen, with new leaves emerging with attractive bronze-red tints in spring before maturing to glossy green. More shade-tolerant than many epimediums and reliably drought-tolerant once established under trees. A superb epimedium species for the woodland garden and difficult dry-shade conditions.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in shade to partial shade; Wushan epimedium is an evergreen species from Chongqing and Sichuan provinces of China, named for the Wushan Gorge area of the Three Gorges. It is one of the most dramatic large-leaved epimediums and exceptionally ornamental.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in shade to partial shade; Wushan epimedium is one of the most dramatic and large-growing epimediums in cultivation, producing very large, evergreen, narrowly ovate leaflets with a fine, spiny-toothed margin and a bold, dark green appearance that persists through winter. The flower display in spring is spectacular — large racemes of pale yellow to soft yellow flowers with slightly reflexed outer petals and long, curved, slightly orange-tipped spurs, born on tall, airy stems well above the foliage. One of the largest-growing and most vigorous epimediums, it can form substantial clumps in good woodland soil. The evergreen foliage makes it an excellent year-round groundcover that suppresses weeds effectively even in dry shade under trees. The cultivar 'Caramel' is particularly sought after for its rich caramel-bronze new growth.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in humus-rich, well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in shade to partial shade; Franchet's epimedium is a vigorous Chinese species with unusually spidery, long-spurred flowers in orange-yellow tones that are striking in the spring garden.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in shade to partial shade; Franchet's epimedium is a Chinese species from Hubei and Sichuan with distinctively spidery, long-spurred flowers in warm orange-yellow tones that stand out among the generally purple and white epimediums. The long, curved, orange-tinged spurs give the flowers a dramatic, airy quality when backlit. The foliage is semi-evergreen to evergreen, with attractive bronzy-red mottling on new spring growth. This species is notable for being one of the more vigorous and spreading epimediums, forming good groundcover more quickly than some of the slower-growing species. It is named for the French botanist Adrien René Franchet, who described many plants introduced from China during the late 19th century. Excellent for dry shade under deciduous trees where it competes well with tree roots.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring as shoots emerge or in fall in humus-rich, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in shade to partial shade; kikutii hosta is a Japanese species prized by hosta specialists for its distinctive narrow, arching leaves and unusually late, prolific flowering in mid-to-late summer.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in shade to partial shade; kikutii hosta is a species hosta from southern Japan distinguished by notably narrow, channeled, deep green leaves that arch gracefully outward from the center of the clump — quite unlike the broad, flat leaves of most popular hosta cultivars. The plant flowers prolifically and later than most hostas, producing tall scapes bearing pure white, narrowly tubular flowers in August and September. Several interesting varieties exist, including var. caput-avis (with green-striped flower buds resembling a bird's head) and var. polyneuron (with exceptionally prominent veining). A collector's hosta particularly valued for its late-season flowering when most hostas have finished blooming. The narrow foliage type adds textural diversity to shaded borders dominated by broad-leaved hostas.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring as shoots emerge or in fall in humus-rich, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in shade to partial shade; nigrescens hosta is a large, bold species from Japan that is a parent of many popular large blue-gray hosta cultivars including 'Krossa Regal'. It develops into an impressive specimen over several years.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in shade to partial shade; nigrescens hosta is a large, architectural species from Japan notable for producing unusually tall, upright, vase-shaped clumps — the leaves are held more erect than most hostas, giving the plant a formal, candlestick silhouette quite different from the typical mounding hosta habit. The large, gray-green leaves have a distinctive glaucous bloom and bold corrugation. The flower scapes are exceptionally tall, rising 4–5 feet above the foliage in mid-summer with lavender flowers. This species is an important parent of many prized large blue cultivars including the classic 'Krossa Regal'. In zone 9, hostas appreciate afternoon shade and consistent moisture through summer heat. An impressive, long-lived specimen hosta for large shade gardens and woodland borders.
